coredata.dc:title: Fusing coupled degradation mechanisms with machine learning: A multi-fidelity framework for lithium-ion battery lifespan prediction

Ren, Fei

coredata.dc:description: Accurate prediction of battery remaining useful life (RUL) is critical for safety enhancement and service extension. However, conventional model-based methods suffer from computational complexity, while data-driven methods struggle with inadequate mechanistic interpretability and prohibitive data requirements. Moreover, RUL prediction in real-world applications often faces the challenge of data sparsity, where only limited early-cycle measurements are available due to time and cost constraints. To address these limitations, we propose a multi-fidelity framework that synergistically integrates electrochemical degradation mechanisms with sparse early-life data, achieving precise RUL predictions through physics-informed interpretability and reduced experimental data. First, we develop an enhanced pseudo two-dimensional (P2D) model that integrates solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) growth mechanisms with partially reversible lithium plating dynamics. This advancement captures nonlinear capacity degradation patterns frequently overlooked in existing models. Second, through causative-process analysis of battery aging trajectories, three governing parameters—SEI solvent diffusivity, lithium plating kinetic rate constant, and dead Li decay constant are identified. Quantitative correlations between molecular-scale processes and macroscopic degradation patterns are established. Crucially, a multi-fidelity regression architecture is designed to simultaneously predict knee point and RUL by adaptively fusing sparse capacity measurements with physics-based degradation trajectories. Validated across 169 commercial cells under different fast-charging protocols, the proposed framework achieves satisfactory accuracy. It maintains Mean Absolute Percentage Errors (MAPE) of just 5.7% for the prediction of knee point and RUL. These results can serve as valuable metrics for performance evaluation and safety improvement of battery systems.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Despite their interpretability and theoretical rigor, model-based approaches often require extensive domain knowledge and complex parameter identification, which can limit their scalability in practical applications. Consequently, data-driven methods have gained increasing attention for their ability to extract degradation patterns directly from operational data without relying on explicit physical modeling [18,19]. Modern implementations leverage diverse statistical learning paradigms \u2013 including Support Vector Machines (SVM) [20], Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) [21,22], Random Forests (RF) [23], and Neural Networks (NN) [24,25]- to autonomously extract degradation features and project battery lifespan. A seminal advancement by Chen et al. [26] introduced an attention-enhanced multi-neural network architecture for SOH/RUL prediction, employing Pauta-criterion-based outlier rejection and multivariate feature engineering. This framework uniquely captured both longitudinal capacity decay trends and transient regeneration phenomena across cycling conditions. Yang et al. [27] introduced a data compression method with Transformer for onboard battery capacity prediction. Further innovating feature extraction, Hsu et al. [28] integrated deep Taylor decomposition with hierarchical neural networks to isolate critical degradation signatures for RUL estimation. Additionally, Lin et al. [29] extracted five voltage-capacity features and used a multilayer perceptron for the prediction of battery knee point across chemistries. Addressing prediction uncertainty, Hu et al. [30] developed a particle-filter-LSTM architecture that synergizes Monte Carlo sampling with sequential learning, achieving narrower confidence interval width.This research has been supported by the Regional Joint Fund of the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant U23A20327, National Natural Science Foundation of China (62333013, 62303278) and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2023QF075).

This research has been supported by the Regional Joint Fund of the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant U23A20327, National Natural Science Foundation of China (62333013, 62303278) and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province , China (ZR2023QF075).

head.ce:title: Fusing coupled degradation mechanisms with machine learning: A multi-fidelity framework for lithium-ion battery lifespan prediction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate prediction of battery remaining useful life (RUL) is critical for safety enhancement and service extension. However, conventional model-based methods suffer from computational complexity, while data-driven methods struggle with inadequate mechanistic interpretability and prohibitive data requirements. Moreover, RUL prediction in real-world applications often faces the challenge of data sparsity, where only limited early-cycle measurements are available due to time and cost constraints. To address these limitations, we propose a multi-fidelity framework that synergistically integrates electrochemical degradation mechanisms with sparse early-life data, achieving precise RUL predictions through physics-informed interpretability and reduced experimental data. First, we develop an enhanced pseudo two-dimensional (P2D) model that integrates solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) growth mechanisms with partially reversible lithium plating dynamics. This advancement captures nonlinear capacity degradation patterns frequently overlooked in existing models. Second, through causative-process analysis of battery aging trajectories, three governing parameters—SEI solvent diffusivity, lithium plating kinetic rate constant, and dead Li decay constant are identified. Quantitative correlations between molecular-scale processes and macroscopic degradation patterns are established. Crucially, a multi-fidelity regression architecture is designed to simultaneously predict knee point and RUL by adaptively fusing sparse capacity measurements with physics-based degradation trajectories. Validated across 169 commercial cells under different fast-charging protocols, the proposed framework achieves satisfactory accuracy. It maintains Mean Absolute Percentage Errors (MAPE) of just 5.7% for the prediction of knee point and RUL. These results can serve as valuable metrics for performance evaluation and safety improvement of battery systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have emerged as the predominant energy storage solution, finding extensive applications in portable electronics, electric vehicles (EVs), and energy storage systems (ESSs), owing to their exceptional cycle life, superior energy density, and significant contributions to decarbonization initiatives [1,2]. As demand for green transportation grows and EV applications expand, increasingly stringent requirements should be met to ensure the long-term performance and safety of LIBs.

As batteries age, their capacity inevitably degrades due to irreversible electrochemical reactions. This degradation typically progresses gradually during the early stage but may accelerate sharply beyond a certain inflection point-commonly referred to as the ”knee point”, after which performance deteriorates rapidly and safety risks increase. Accurate prediction of the remaining useful life (RUL), which defines how long a battery can operate before reaching this critical threshold, is therefore essential for battery health management and safety assurance [3,4]. In addition, in practical scenarios, RUL estimation is often based on data collected during early battery life, when degradation signals are still weak and less informative. This results in a challenging issue of data sparsity, particularly before the knee point, where the degradation trajectory has not yet fully manifested. The scarcity of representative degradation patterns during early cycles significantly limits the effectiveness of conventional regression models, making it difficult to capture future aging trends or anticipate the onset of rapid failure.

Researchers have conducted extensive studies on RUL prediction, most of which rely on continuously collected degradation data. These efforts have led to the development of three major categories of approaches: model-based methods, data-driven methods, and hybrid methods [4,5]. Among these, model-based methods characterize the dynamic evolution of battery's internal aging states through mathematical formulations. Depending on modeling principles, these methods are further classified into empirical models and physical models. As quintessential time-series modeling framework, empirical models quantify the degradation patterns of battery capacity and other aging metrics across charge-discharge cycles using explicit mathematical functions [6–8]. For instance, Meng et al. [9] innovatively integrated Perceptually Important Points (PIP) with partial charging data to construct an empirical model for early-stage degradation prediction, effectively addressing challenge of limited cycling data in LIB applications. To enhance model adaptability, researchers often couple empirical models with state estimation algorithms for dynamic parameter updating [10]. Notably, empirical models demonstrate deployment advantages in computationally constrained scenarios, yet their prediction accuracy becomes significantly compromised under complex operating conditions. However, their reliance on fixed functional forms limits their ability to capture complex, nonlinear degradation patterns, particularly under variable operating conditions or fast-charging scenarios, where predictive accuracy may degrade significantly.

To overcome these limitations, physical models have been developed to establish mechanistic correlations between internal electrochemical processes and observable degradation patterns. By incorporating domain knowledge and physicochemical laws, physical models offer improved interpretability and generalization across different usage profiles. A prominent example is the equivalent circuit model (ECM), constructed by mapping distinct electrical components to specific internal electrochemical processes [11]. This framework enables researchers to leverage established circuit analysis techniques for deciphering battery degradation mechanisms [12]. Crucially, aging behaviors manifest as measurable parameter drift within these models. Illustrating this paradigm, Kang et al. [13] devised a prognostic framework for retired LIBs, combining a simplified ECM with logarithmic cycling equations. By extracting electrochemical time constants from the initial 20 cycles and categorizing cells into high/medium/low-grade clusters based on resistance and residual capacity, they achieved <5% prediction errors for high/medium-grade batteries.

While ECMs simplify battery aging characterization by modeling parametric shifts, they often overlook the complex interdependencies among internal state variables. As a result, ECM-based approaches may inadequately capture the multi-physics coupling effects that govern battery behavior under dynamic operating conditions. To enhance the mechanistic interpretability of aging processes, electrochemical models (EMs) have been established, which directly incorporate the underlying physicochemical reactions driving degradation. These models enable the identification of more representative aging indicators that go beyond conventional metrics such as capacity or resistance. In particular, the formation and evolution of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) result in irreversible lithium inventory loss and resistance growth, while lithium plating further accelerates degradation through coupled interactions. By explicitly modeling these internal processes, EMs offer deeper insight into battery health evolution and support more accurate RUL prediction under varying operational conditions [14]. Advancing this understanding, Liu et al. [15] demonstrated the efficacy of pseudo-two-dimensional (P2D) modeling for quantifying SEI kinetics in RUL prognostics. A paradigm-shifting contribution comes from Kim et al. [16], who introduced a physics-based inhomogeneous stress-induced fracture model integrating electrochemical-mechanical coupling to predict capacity fade, explicitly capturing nonlinear phenomena like cathode particle fracturing and abrupt knee point degradation transitions. Methodologically distinct, Lv et al. [17] developed a two-stage EM framework that segments battery aging into two phases: a linear phase dominated by SEI layer growth, and an accelerated nonlinear phase governed by lithium plating dynamics. The primary advantage of EMs lies in their direct mechanistic interpretability – degradation processes are rigorously described through first-principles physicochemical equations. However, EM implementations face formidable computational demands due to their governing coupled partial differential equations and sophisticated parameter optimization. Notably, current research on coupling mechanisms of SEI and lithium plating remains confined to fundamental mechanistic investigations and has yet to be operationalized for practical RUL prediction systems.

Despite their interpretability and theoretical rigor, model-based approaches often require extensive domain knowledge and complex parameter identification, which can limit their scalability in practical applications. Consequently, data-driven methods have gained increasing attention for their ability to extract degradation patterns directly from operational data without relying on explicit physical modeling [18,19]. Modern implementations leverage diverse statistical learning paradigms – including Support Vector Machines (SVM) [20], Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) [21,22], Random Forests (RF) [23], and Neural Networks (NN) [24,25]- to autonomously extract degradation features and project battery lifespan. A seminal advancement by Chen et al. [26] introduced an attention-enhanced multi-neural network architecture for SOH/RUL prediction, employing Pauta-criterion-based outlier rejection and multivariate feature engineering. This framework uniquely captured both longitudinal capacity decay trends and transient regeneration phenomena across cycling conditions. Yang et al. [27] introduced a data compression method with Transformer for onboard battery capacity prediction. Further innovating feature extraction, Hsu et al. [28] integrated deep Taylor decomposition with hierarchical neural networks to isolate critical degradation signatures for RUL estimation. Additionally, Lin et al. [29] extracted five voltage-capacity features and used a multilayer perceptron for the prediction of battery knee point across chemistries. Addressing prediction uncertainty, Hu et al. [30] developed a particle-filter-LSTM architecture that synergizes Monte Carlo sampling with sequential learning, achieving narrower confidence interval width.

Although data-driven models offer strong nonlinear mapping capabilities, their performance is often limited by two critical factors: a strong dependence on large, high-quality training datasets and a lack of physical interpretability. To bridge the gap between mechanistic accuracy and statistical flexibility, hybrid methodologies have emerged as a promising direction in RUL prediction. These approaches aim to integrate physics-based knowledge with data-driven adaptability, thereby mitigating the limitations of using either strategy alone. To address the robustness challenges of physics-based models under variable loading conditions, Han et al. [31] engineered a dual-phase framework merging a semi-empirical capacity decay model with particle filtering, enabling concurrent SOH estimation and RUL prediction. Recognizing the limitations of pure data-driven methods in handling capacity regeneration phenomena, Chen et al. [32] devised a grey-NN cascade architecture, where a sliding-window grey model first approximates capacity trajectories, which are then refined via backpropagation neural networks for uncertainty-aware forecasting. Pioneering ensemble learning, Lin et al. [33] ensembled three base learners—multiple linear regression, SVR, and GPR-through Bayesian optimization-weighted fusion, achieving a great improvement in prediction robustness over single-model baselines. Tu et al. [34] developed a hybrid architecture that integrated EM with feedback neural network (FNN). The FNN was informed by the internal state of the EM to correct missing information gaps in comparison to the actual battery physics.

These model-based, data-driven, and hybrid approaches have collectively advanced the development of RUL prediction. As battery usage scenarios grow increasingly complex-particularly with the widespread adoption of fast charging, there is a growing recognition within the researchers of the importance of leveraging early-cycle capacity data to investigate and anticipate the onset of capacity diving. This shift in focus underscores the need for predictive methods that can operate effectively under limited-data conditions and enable timely intervention before rapid degradation occurs. To facilitate early detection of nonlinear degradation transitions across diverse battery chemistries, Jia et al. [35] incorporated electrode level aging mechanisms into random forest regressors. In Ref. [36], they developed a mechanistic decision tree framework that directly utilized SEI growth and lithium plating indicators as model inputs. Furthermore, several studies have further explored the feasibility of deep learning-based RUL prediction using early-cycle data [37,38]. Fei et al. [39,40] proposed transformer-based architectures, convolutional autoencoders, and hybrid feature engineering strategies to effectively extract latent degradation patterns from limited early-cycle information, achieving high predictive accuracy across diverse operating conditions.

Nevertheless, most of these methods rely on densely sampled early-cycle data, assuming access to continuous capacity measurements during the initial degradation phase. In practice, however, capacity data is typically available only at discrete inspection intervals or specific cycles, resulting in a sparse and incomplete early-cycle dataset. On the other hand, the physical models used in existing architecture lack explicit representation of the coupled mechanisms of SEI growth, lithium plating and dead Li formation, that drive nonlinear battery capacity diving. To address these research gaps, this work introduces a mechanistically enhanced P2D aging model that explicitly couples SEI growth, lithium plating and dead Li formation, bridging the gap between fundamental degradation mechanisms and predictive modeling. By integrating coupled aging mechanisms with early-life sparse capacity data, the proposed method enables accurate prediction of battery degradation knee points and RUL. The main contributions of this paper are as follows:
1.
By establishing a mechanistically enhanced P2D model that simultaneously resolves SEI growth dynamics, lithium plating and dead Li accumulation, this work advances battery degradation modeling through explicit coupling of these synergistic aging mechanisms. The coupled electrochemical degradation model enables unprecedented tracking of nonlinear capacity fade trajectories arising from the competitive interplay between electrode passivation and metallic lithium formation.
2.
The specific ways of how these three key parameters, namely SEI solvent diffusivity, lithium plating kinetic rate constant, and dead Li decay constant, influence battery capacity decay at different stages are analyzed in detail. This tripartite hierarchy establishes quantifiable links between microscopic degradation processes and macroscopic aging signatures.
3.
A novel multi-fidelity framework that synergistically integrates a physics-constrained degradation model with sparse early-cycle operational data is proposed for battery RUL prediction. Unlike traditional data-driven approaches that rely on dense long-term measurements, our method requires only early-cycle capacity observations to achieve clinically relevant prognostic accuracy. It successfully identifies capacity knee points and predicts the RUL across a diverse dataset of 169 batteries. This innovation addresses the long-standing challenge of performance prediction under data-scarce conditions while maintaining strong electrochemical interpretability.

The remainder of this paper is structured as follows: Section 2 delineates a detailed description of the experimental datasets employed in this study; Section 3 systematically elucidates a comprehensive exposition of the proposed methodology; Section 4 rigorously analyzes the prediction results with comparative discussions; Finally, the conclusion is given in Section 5.

The cycling aging analysis in this study leverages two high-fidelity, publicly accessible datasets including 169 batteries provided by a collaborative team from Stanford University and the Massachusetts Institute of Technology [41]. These datasets are specifically designed to investigate lithium-ion battery degradation under realistic fast-charging conditions. Dataset A comprises aging trajectories of 45 commercial lithium-ion cells, subjected to nine distinct multi-stage constant-current (CC) fast-charging protocols (Case 1–9), each denoted as “CC1–CC2–CC3–CC4–CC5 (1C)” and discharged at a consistent 4C rate at 30 °C. The experimental protocols are detailed in Fig. 1(b), and corresponding specifications are summarized in Table 1 . This dataset captures cycle life spans ranging from 400 to 1,200 cycles, with deliberate incorporation of cell-to-cell variability to reflect practical operational conditions, as illustrated in Fig. 1(a)–(c).

Dataset B extends this study by including 124 commercial lithium-ion cells tested under consistent fast-charging protocols but acquired at three different experimental time periods (Cases 10–12). In Dataset B, the detailed fast-charging protocols are provided in Table 2 . At a constant ambient temperature of 30 °C, all cells are charged using 72 distinct fast-charging profiles, denoted as “CC1–CC2”, where CC1 represents the initial constant current and CC2 indicates the second-stage current applied after a predefined state of charge is reached. Charging is terminated at 80% SOC during the fast-charging phase, after which all cells undergo a standard 1C constant-current constant-voltage charging process until fully charged. Voltage limits are consistently set at 3.6 and 2.0 V across all charging stages, following the cell manufacturer's specifications. All discharge operations are carried out at a fixed rate of 4C. This expanded dataset further broadens the observed cycle life range to 400–2,000 cycles, offering a more comprehensive picture of battery aging behavior across a wider distribution of manufacturing batches. The corresponding aging curves and cycle life distributions for representative cases are shown in Fig. 2(a)–(f). The extended cycling range provides critical insights into both gradual capacity fade mechanisms and abrupt nonlinear degradation phenomena, while simultaneously posing unique challenges for RUL prediction. The 1,600-cycle variability window between earliest and latest EOL occurrences necessitates robust modeling frameworks capable of reconciling inter-cell heterogeneity with underlying degradation physics—a critical requirement for industrial battery management systems.

In this work, a novel multi-fidelity prediction framework integrating physics-based model and machine learning is proposed. The overview of the framework is shown in Fig. 3 . The framework begins with the current profiles obtained from real-world battery applications, which are used as inputs to a modified P2D battery aging model. This model is capable of generating continuous capacity degradation data by incorporating the coupled effects of SEI growth, lithium plating, and dead Li accumulation. The degradation behavior is governed by three key parameters: SEI solvent diffusivity, lithium plating rate constant, and dead Li decay constant, which collectively determine the nonlinear aging characteristics of the battery.

In practical scenarios, however, only limited early-cycle capacity data are typically available due to operational constraints. To bridge the gap between the continuous simulation output and sparse experimental measurements, we introduce a dynamic temporal alignment strategy based on Dynamic Time Warping (DTW). By minimizing the DTW distance between the simulated and measured degradation trajectories through grid search, the model parameters are calibrated such that the simulated curves closely match the morphology of the real-world early degradation patterns.

Once a physically consistent simulation is obtained, the proposed framework integrates simulation outputs and experimental data within a unified co-Kriging structure. The low-fidelity simulation outputs are first modeled by a primary GPR, and the high-fidelity data are used to train a secondary discrepancy model that captures the residual error between simulation and real degradation. This hierarchical design enables uncertainty-aware and data-efficient RUL prediction. Further computational details and implementation procedures are described in the following sections.

The degradation model proposed in this work is developed based on the classic P2D model, which provides a detailed electrochemical description of lithium-ion batteries but does not explicitly account for long-term capacity fade [42]. To address this limitation, we introduce a modified P2D-based framework that integrates three key degradation mechanisms: SEI growth, lithium plating, and dead Li formation. While each of these mechanisms has been studied individually in the literature, the proposed model is among the first to consider their coupling interactions within a unified electrochemical framework. This coupling allows us to capture the mutual influence among different degradation pathways, which is critical for accurately simulating long-term capacity loss.

In addition, given any experimentally measured current input, the model is capable of continuously computing the evolution of battery capacity over time. This enables the generation of high-resolution capacity degradation data that reflect actual usage patterns. These model-generated degradation data will later be fused with true aging datasets to construct a hybrid framework for RUL prediction. The proposed degradation model not only enhances physical interpretability but also serves as a reliable source of virtual aging data to support long-term prognostics of lithium-ion batteries.

In the P2D model, the battery's internal architecture is conceptualized through two distinct phases (solid and electrolyte) and three principal regions (positive electrode, negative electrode, and separator). Each region is mathematically characterized by a set of coupled partial differential equations (PDEs) that govern the electrochemical processes. Specifically, both positive and negative electrodes are modeled as composite structures consisting of a continuous electrolyte phase and numerous uniformly distributed spherical active particles, wherein Li+ transport is predominantly governed by radial diffusion mechanisms. It has been generally acknowledged that the growth of SEI layer consumes Li+ and causes the loss of battery capacity. During the battery charging process, four reactions could occur on the electrode surface: lithium-ion intercalation, SEI growth, lithium plating and stripping, as shown in Fig. 4 . The most ideal electrochemical reaction is the complete intercalation of Li+ into the carbon anode, following Eq. (10):

The SEI growth model has been widely studied by many researchers and literature has not formed a uniform consensus on the most accurate expression. Safari et al. [43] studied different expressions and found that diffusion-limited model provides the best fit to experimental data, which is also utilized in this work. However, most previous studies assumed that the plated Li metal is either fully reversible or irreversible. In fact, upon subsequent discharge, a portion of this deposited lithium undergoes electrochemical oxidation, dissolving back into Li+ through the lithium stripping process. Not all plated lithium participates in this reversible reaction, so Eq. (18) should be revised as:

And the increasing SEI thickness makes Li+ stripping more difficult, leading to more plated Li being converted into dead Li. To describe this dependence, a simplified exponential equation is used to model this process:

where λ 0 is defined as dead Li decay constant, which is the key parameter determining the Li+ loss caused by the dead Li formation.

Based on the analysis of battery capacity degradation mechanisms above, the primary internal side reactions influencing battery aging include SEI growth and lithium plating. The coupling mechanisms between SEI growth, lithium plating, and dead Li formation are illustrated in Fig. 5 . Three key parameters determining these reactions are SEI solvent diffusivity, lithium plating kinetic rate constant, and dead Li decay constant. Additional model parameters are primarily sourced from references, as provided in Table 3 . In particular, both SEI growth and the formation of dead Li induced by irreversible lithium plating lead to a continuous loss of active Li+, which directly results in battery capacity fade. By adjusting the key degradation parameters, the model can generate continuous and physically meaningful capacity degradation sequences. This capability lays a solid foundation for subsequent model-data alignment and parameter calibration, which will be described in detail in the following section.

DTW establishes a nonlinear mapping between A and B by optimally warping the time axis to minimize cumulative alignment error. This approach achieves two primary objectives: (1) synchronization of characteristic points in the degradation trajectories, and (2) minimization of the cumulative distance between the aligned sequences. In doing so, DTW effectively resolves the dimensional and temporal mismatch between discrete experimental measurements and continuous model outputs, while preserving the underlying degradation patterns.

The DTW computation is formally implemented through two sequential operations: (1) construction of cumulative distance matrices and (2) identification of optimal warping paths. A pairwise distance matrix D ∈
ℝ

Then, the parameter optimization process initiates by calculating the DTW distance between discrete experimental data and the simulated degradation curve. A grid search method iteratively adjusts three mechanistically critical parameters (SEI solvent diffusivity, lithium plating kinetic rate constant, dead Li decay constant) to minimize the DTW metric. The optimization terminates when DTW < 0.2, which is an empirically derived value. While the resulting model achieves only moderate absolute accuracy (insufficient for direct remaining useful life prediction), this intentional parsimony balances computational efficiency with first-order trend alignment. Critically, the limited parameterization sacrifices comprehensive electrochemical reconstruction capability, yet successfully preserves early-stage aging signatures through DTW-guided trajectory alignment. This physics-aware approximation establishes a robust foundation for subsequent multi-fidelity prediction frameworks by enforcing consistency between mechanism-driven simulations and empirical degradation patterns.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-fidelity RUL prediction based on Gaussian Process Regression
ce:section.ce:para: Despite the DTW-based calibration of the three key degradation parameters, many other parameters in the modified P2D model remain uncalibrated due to experimental constraints and limited observability. As a result, the model-generated capacity degradation sequence B can only approximate the general degradation trend and is still regarded as low-fidelity in nature. Accordingly, this section introduces a multi-fidelity prediction framework based on GPR, which integrates the low-fidelity simulation output B with the high-fidelity experimental data A . By systematically combining physics-informed predictions with sparse but accurate measurements, the proposed method aims to improve the accuracy and robustness of RUL prediction while reducing reliance on extensive experimental aging data.

The framework relies on two core components, namely GPR and autoregressive stochastic schemes [48]. GPR, as a non-parametric Bayesian approach, is particularly suitable for this task due to its strong generalization capability and intrinsic probabilistic nature. It provides not only accurate point predictions but also uncertainty estimates, which are essential for reliability assessment in scenarios involving sparse high-fidelity data. Moreover, GPR enables multi-fidelity learning through its natural extension to co-Kriging structures, allowing effective integration of low-fidelity simulations and high-fidelity experimental observations within an autoregressive modeling framework.

Building upon a hierarchical integration mechanism, the GPR framework is extended to establish co-Kriging-based multi-fidelity modeling for seamless assimilation of heterogeneous data across accuracy hierarchies [48]. In this context, the model-generated degradation sequence B serves as the low-fidelity data source, while the experimentally measured capacity data A represents the high-fidelity observations. To more closely reflect real-world conditions, where battery capacity calibration is typically conducted at periodic intervals rather than continuously, the training set uses sparse data within the first 300 cycles from both datasets.

The architecture employs dual-layer Gaussian processes: (1) a computationally inexpensive low-fidelity model approximating baseline degradation behavior based on B , and (2) a discrepancy-corrected high-fidelity model that leverages the sparse but accurate information in A to refine predictions, as governed by Eq. (34). The predictions synthesize probabilistic outputs from the LF model G low, and a bias-correcting Kriging surrogate G diff(x), which explicitly quantifies systematic inter-fidelity deviations and associated uncertainties. So the autoregressive scheme can be written as:
(34)
G CoK
( x )
= ρ
G low
( x )
+
G diff
( x )
where ρ represents a dimensionless scaling factor. This allows the autoregressive scheme realize the nonlinear multi-fidelity information fusion.

Both the low- and high-fidelity gaussian processes adopt squared exponential kernels with independently initialized hyperparameters. The training dataset is structured as X = [ x , f], where f ∈ {0, 1} indicates the fidelity level. Prior to training, all input features and outputs are normalized. The model is optimized using the L-BFGS algorithm with 10 random restarts to reduce the risk of convergence to local minima. The inter-fidelity correlation coefficient ρ, as well as kernel hyperparameters, are jointly estimated by maximizing the marginal log-likelihood. The number of training samples for each fidelity level is determined based on the total number of aging cycles and the chosen sampling interval.

The low-fidelity data is firstly trained by a GPR recursive framework, and then the high-fidelity Kriging predictor can be calculated by:

The basic battery P2D model is implemented using Pybamm, an open-source battery modeling software package developed in Python by Oxford University based on Python [49]. The software is designed with extensibility and modularity in mind, enabling seamless integration of new sub-models and numerical methods. In our work, models for the processes of SEI growth, lithium plating, and stripping are developed and integrated into the source code.

Fig. 7 shows the typical battery capacity retention curve, where the failure threshold for battery capacity is set at 80%. The definition of the knee point follows the method in Ref. [50]. The capacity degradation curve can generally be divided into two stages, with the knee point as the boundary. In the first stage, capacity degradation is primarily driven by the growth of the SEI layer, leading to a relatively slow decline. In the second stage, the formation of dead Li due to lithium plating results in an exponential drop in the Loss of Lithium Inventory (LLI), causing a rapid capacity diving. The X knee represents the cycle number of knee point, while the Y knee represents the corresponding capacity. The Cycend represents the cycle number at the EoL. Therefore, the coordinates of the knee point (X knee, Y knee) and Cycend can basically determine the general trend of the capacity retention curve, and they are extracted as curve features for the following analysis.

To investigate the specific impact of the SEI solvent diffusivity, lithium plating kinetic rate constant, and dead Li decay constant on battery capacity degradation, other model parameters are set to default values, and only these three parameters are tuned carefully. Since these coupling-related parameters have rarely been considered in previous electrochemical models, their initial values are not readily available from the literature. Therefore, we adopted a trial-and-adjustment approach: the parameters were manually tuned within physically reasonable ranges, and the resulting model outputs were compared with typical battery degradation curves to identify appropriate intervals. The initial value of D sol, k plt and λ 0 are set as: 2.5e-22, 1e-11 and 1e-7, respectively. Subsequent Pearson correlation analysis between these parameters and capacity fade trajectories quantitatively determines their statistical significance in governing both knee point emergence and RUL evolution.

As shown in Fig. 8 (a), the increase of solvent diffusivity accelerates the overall rate of battery capacity fade significantly, leading to the battery capacity reaching the failure threshold more rapidly. Specifically, the D sol increase can facilitate the reaction speed of Li+ and the electrolyte solvent at the anode particle surface, resulting in the accumulated SEI growth. Consequently, a significant amount of Li+ react to form the SEI layer during the first stage, thereby accelerating LLI in the early stage. The originally gradual decline trend transforms progressively steeper. Fig. 8(d) shows the correlation coefficient between D sol and X knee exceeds −0.99, also indicating that the increment of D sol promotes the transition of the two aging stages. The impedance caused by SEI layer growth can lead to a gradual decline of anode potential. When the anode potential drops below 0 V vs Li+/Li during the charging process, lithium plating begins to occur. However, the correlation coefficient between X knee and Cycend is 0.98, indicating a stable linear relationship. This suggests the capacity decay rate in the second phase remains relatively stable under fixed dead Li decay constants, as most plated Li is re-intercalated into the electrode particles through stripping processes.

Fig. 8 b) illustrates the impact of lithium plating kinetic constant k
plt on battery capacity degradation trajectory. The k
plt primarily regulates the extent of the plating reaction of Eq. (21) during charging process, and a larger k
plt leads to higher j
plt . During the first aging stage, the increased plating current density causes a larger quantity of Li+ inventory to participate in Li metal deposition. But lithium plating is less prevalent at this stage due to the anode potential exceed 0V vs Li+ under most conditions, which is the reason why correlation coefficient between k
plt and X
knee is only −0.95 in Fig. 8 e). In addition, although the overall proportion of Li+ intercalation into the electrode particles remains invariable when dead Li decay constant is unchanged, the quantity of generated dead Li is increasing. This implies that the LLI caused by plating dominates the capacity loss earlier, leading to a steeper capacity fade curve.

The impact of the dead Li decay constant λ 0 on battery capacity degradation trajectory is shown in Fig. 8 c). As discussed before, most of the Li+ can re-intercalation into particles in stripping process after lithium plating, and the λ 0 determines the proportion of generated dead Li after lithium plating. Unlike that in Fig. 8 d) and 8 e), Y
knee is directly proportional to the dead li decay constant, with a correlation coefficient of 0.98 in Fig. 8 f). This is because a higher λ 0 causes most of the plated lithium metal to be converted into dead Li, allowing the capacity degradation to enter the second phase more quickly, which is predominantly governed by lithium plating. As a result, the Y
knee shows an upward trend. Moreover, as battery ages, the SEI thickness and the rate of dead Li formation are both accelerated exponentially, accounting for the capacity diving in the second stage. The coefficient between λ 0 and Cyc
end reach up to −0.99, illustrating the significance of λ 0 on the capacity degradation trend.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RUL and knee point prediction results
ce:section.ce:para:
The dataset used in this study comprises 169 battery cells that can be categorized into 12 cases. The proposed method's performance was evaluated using multiple quantitative metrics: (1) mean absolute error (MAE) in Eq. (42), which quantifies the absolute deviation between predicted and actual values; (2) mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) in Eq. (43), representing the relative prediction accuracy for individual cases; and (3) comprehensive evaluation metrics including mean MAE and mean MAPE, which assess the overall prediction performance across all test cases.
(42)
M A E =
1

i = 1

(43)
M A P E =

× 100 %
where
y
ˆ
i
and y
i represent the predicted value and true value, respectively; z is the number of prediction sample. Considering that battery capacity calibration in practical applications is typically performed at periodic intervals, the acquired capacity data exhibits inherent discontinuity. To emulate real-world operational constraints, the proposed framework strategically employs sparse discrete data from early cycling stages. Specifically, battery capacity data are sampled at 20-cycle intervals within the first 300 cycles. These discrete measurements are integrated with the modified P2D model to enable continuous prediction of capacity fade trajectories and RUL estimation.

The prediction results of battery capacity trajectory under different cases using sparse actual data with 20-cycles interval are shown in Fig. 9 . The blue dots represent the actual sparse capacity data, the green curve shows the actual capacity degradation curve, the light blue curve represents the simulation results, and the red dashed curve shows the predicted curve with 95 % confidence. The black and red dots indicate the true and predicted knee points, respectively. It is not difficult to see that the simulation curve can generally track the true aging trend throughout the lifespan under all 12 cases, indicating the reliability of the established modified P2D model. The simulated data are consistent with the actual cycle data at the initial stage only by adjusting three key parameters. Nevertheless, in Case 1, Case 3, Case 5 and Case-11, the simulation curves show a high degree of agreement with the actual data. However, the performance in other cases is comparatively poorer. Without guidance from the true data, the simulation results clearly deviate from the actual trajectory and the discrepancy gradually increases, especially in Case 2, Case 7 and Case 8. This is because the model simulation results can only fine-tune the three parameters based on early sparse data, so it cannot fully capture the battery capacity degradation trend in the future. In some cases, adjusting these three parameters allow the simulation curve to fit well with the real data. Since many other parameters in the model remain unidentified, the predicted results cannot fully simulate all conditions. In contrast, the multi-fidelity prediction method incorporates aging mechanisms from the model and degradation trends from real data, enabling long-term accurate predictions. When the simulation curve aligns well with the actual curve, the multi-fidelity prediction curve also ensures accurate prediction results, as seen in Case 1, Case 3, Case 5 and Case 11. Moreover, when the simulation curve gradually deviates from the actual values, the multi-fidelity prediction results can utilize the trend from the early real data combined with the battery's nonlinear degradation mechanism to progressively approach the actual results, thereby reducing estimation errors. It is evident that the multi-fidelity prediction results are significantly closer to the actual data in the second stage.

The prediction errors of RUL and knee point in each case are quantified according to the two indicators of MAE and MAPE, as shown in Fig. 10 . For the RUL estimation results, it can be observed that, except for Cases 8 and 9, the MAEs in other cases are less than 50 cycles and the MAPEs are generally below 5 %, indicating that the proposed method demonstrates good adaptability under various conditions. In the case of Case 8 and Case 9, the average charging rates of the battery under these conditions reached 5.42 and 5.78, respectively, which are higher than in other cases. This may have triggered other side electrochemical reactions, causing unexpected capacity loss that is not considered in the model. In these cases, the battery EoL are significantly reduced to around 600 cycles, contributing to the MAPE exceeding 10 %. Regarding the knee point prediction, the MAEs for the knee point position across all conditions are generally below 50, with MAPEs close to 5 %, showing satisfactory accuracy. The knee point estimation errors for Case 8 and Case 9 are similar to that of other cases, but the RUL estimation error is significantly higher, further indicating that additional side reactions likely occurred in the second stage, increasing the uncertainty in predicting the degradation trajectory. Overall, the mean MAPE of the proposed RUL and knee point prediction method under all conditions is only 5.7 %.

Fig. 11 a) initially presents the DTW distances of each cell across all the cases. As a quantitative measure of early-stage degradation pattern alignment between simulation models and empirical data, the DTW values predominantly reside below 0.2, confirming effective capture of initial capacity fade characteristics in most scenarios. Contrary to conventional expectations, reduced DTW values do not linearly correlate with improved prediction accuracy in the multi-fidelity RUL prediction framework. Fig. 11 b) systematically investigates this relationship through Pearson correlation analysis between these metrics: 1) DTW distances, 2) absolute errors (AE) of RUL/knee point predictions, and 3) absolute percentage errors (APE) of RUL/knee point predictions.

Notably, the heatmap reveals moderate correlations (0.5–0.9 range) between DTW and prediction errors, with unexpected negative correlations observed in Case 6 and Case 9. This phenomenon stems from the inherent trade-off in multi-fidelity modeling: Over-optimized DTW values induce excessive reliance on the physical model during data fusion. While achieving near-perfect early-stage alignment by the calibration of only three aging parameters, the low-fidelity model lacks the capacity for full lifecycle extrapolation. Consequently, the prediction system becomes vulnerable to accumulating model bias in later stages, paradoxically reducing final prediction accuracy despite superior short-term matching. The findings establish an optimal DTW operating window that balances early-stage pattern matching with life cycle prediction robustness. This guideline prevents both underfitting and overconfidence risks in battery prognostic frameworks.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison analysis of parameter optimization methods
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the efficiency and effectiveness of different parameter optimization strategies, comparison analysis of the grid search method with two widely used heuristic algorithms: Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) and Genetic Algorithm (GA) is conducted. All three methods are applied to identify optimal three physical parameters that minimize the discrepancy between model-predicted and experimental degradation trajectories, with the DTW distance serving as the evaluation metric. Unlike conventional optimization tasks that aim for global minima, the objective in this study is to identify parameter sets that ensure the DTW distance remains below a threshold of 0.2. As discussed before, excessively reducing DTW may result in overfitting to measured data and suppress the correction capability of real-world observations in the multi-fidelity framework.

As shown in Table 4 , PSO achieves the best performance in terms of accuracy, with a lower MAE for both RUL (39.3) and knee point prediction (34.5). In contrast, GA achieves the lowest MAE for the knee point of 33.2 but results in a slightly higher RUL error than grid search, indicating that it did not consistently outperform the baseline. Moreover, both heuristic methods required more computation time: 1006 s for PSO and 1103 s for GA, compared to only 763 s for grid search.

Given that the improvements in prediction accuracy are marginal and the cost of increased computational complexity and greater sensitivity to algorithmic hyperparameters, grid search is ultimately adopted in this study. Its deterministic nature, implementation simplicity, and satisfactory performance under the relaxed DTW threshold make it an efficient choice for this optimization task.

To systematically evaluate the sensitivity of cycle interval selection on estimation fidelity, a comparative analysis of 40-and 60-cycles interval predictions is performed, as shown in Tables 5 and 6
and Fig. 12 .

As the cycle interval increases from 20 to 60, both RUL and knee point prediction errors exhibit a systematic upward trend. This increase is primarily due to the reduction in real measurement points, which weakens the model's ability to correct the simulated degradation trajectory. In most cases, RUL and knee point errors increase approximately linearly with the interval, reflecting the typical impact of reduced supervision.

When the interval reaches 40 cycles, a noticeable rise in prediction error occurs. Specifically, the average MAE for RUL estimation reaches 63.5 cycles, with most cases exhibiting errors around 50 cycles. The knee point prediction also deteriorates, with an average MAE of 54.4. Compared to the 20-cycle interval, the MAPE increase remains within 5 %, despite the number of true data points being halved. This indicates that, while reduced information density leads to higher errors, the proposed multi-fidelity method retains satisfactory predictive performance under moderate sparsity.

Interestingly, as the interval further increases to 60 cycles, the rate of error growth slows down. And cases 8–9 maintain relatively stable prediction performance from 40 cycles to 60 intervals. This can be attributed to their rapid capacity degradation and truncated end-of-life trajectories, which inherently limit long-term divergence and contribute to greater robustness in knee point estimation. Generally, both RUL and knee point MAEs rise by fewer than 10 cycles relative to the 40-cycle scenario, and the corresponding MAPE values increase by only about 1 %. This suggests that the underlying physical model begins to play a more dominant role in constraining the prediction trajectory, partially compensating for the lack of measurement data.

Beyond error magnitude, data sparsity also affects the variability of prediction results across different cases. As the interval exceeds 40 cycles, a divergence emerges in the prediction trajectories, indicating that increased sparsity introduces greater uncertainty. This is characterized by both elevated baseline errors and more dispersed outcomes across battery cells. Notably, several cases exhibit MAPE values exceeding 10 % for either RUL or knee point prediction, signaling a decline in prediction reliability beyond this level. This phenomenon suggests that a 40-cycle interval may serve as an empirical sparsity threshold, beyond which the model's reliance on prior physics becomes insufficient to ensure consistent and accurate predictions.

Precise battery RUL estimation constitutes a critical enabler for risk mitigation and service lifetime extension in LIBs applications. This study proposes a novel multi-fidelity prediction framework that integrates physics-based model and machine learning. An enhanced P2D model coupling SEI growth dynamics with partially reversible lithium plating mechanisms, explicitly capturing nonlinear capacity fade patterns critical for knee point and RUL prediction. By coupling SEI growth with partially reversible lithium plating, a more comprehensive analysis of the coupled mechanisms driving battery capacity degradation is achieved. Through the correlation analysis in model simulation, three key parameters, namely SEI solvent diffusion coefficient, lithium plating kinetic rate constant, and dead Li decay constant—are identified as critical to determining the battery's capacity degradation trajectory. Following this, a multi-fidelity regression method is proposed by integrating the modified mechanistic model with sparse experimental cycling data for accurate battery aging trajectory prediction. Utilizing only early-stage discrete capacity data, the method successfully predicts the capacity knee points and RUL for 169 batteries, with prediction MAPE values of both 5.7 % for the knee point and RUL. This study lays the foundation for enhancing the reliability and safety of battery systems, supporting broader applications in electric vehicles and energy storage technologies.

While the presented results are promising, further investigation is needed to clarify the limitations and generalizability of the proposed approach. Future work will examine the model's sensitivity to data sparsity, distribution, and manufacturing batch variations. Although a 40-cycle interval threshold is identified in this study, its applicability across different chemistries remains uncertain. In addition, the impact of unmodeled side reactions, such as particle crack or electrolyte decomposition, on prediction accuracy warrants further study. Systematic evaluations under diverse degradation modes, noise levels, and missing data patterns will help improve the model's robustness and real-world applicability.

Dong Lu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Fei Ren: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Changlong Li: Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis. Haoyu Ming: Visualization, Validation, Data curation. Naxin Cui: Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
The data and materials used to support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
